title: Ensure GKE clusters are not running using the Compute  Engine default service account

input_row.title: Ensure GKE clusters are not running using the Compute  Engine default service account
input_row.description: Create and use minimally privileged Service accounts to run GKE cluster nodes instead of using the Compute Engine default Service account. Unnecessary permissions could be abused in the case of a node compromise.
input_row.rationale: A GCP service account (as distinct from a Kubernetes ServiceAccount) is an identity that an instance or an application can be used to run GCP API requests. This identity is used to identify virtual machine instances to other Google Cloud Platform services. By default, Kubernetes Engine nodes use the Compute Engine default service account. This account has broad access by default, as defined by access scopes, making it useful to a wide variety of applications on the VM, but it has more permissions than are required to run your Kubernetes Engine cluster. A minimally privileged service account should be created and used to run the Kubernetes Engine cluster instead of using the Compute Engine default service account, and create separate service accounts for each Kubernetes Workload (See recommendation 5.2.2). Kubernetes Engine requires, at a minimum, the node service account to have the monitoring.viewer, monitoring.metricWriter, and logging.logWriter roles. Additional roles may need to be added for the nodes to pull images from GCR.
input_row.audit: Using Google Cloud Console: Internal Only - General 1. Go to Kubernetes Engine by visiting https://console.cloud.google.com/kubernetes/list 2. Select the cluster under test and click on each Node pool to bring up the Node pool details page. Ensure that for each Node pool the Service account is not set to default under the Security heading. To check the permissions allocated to the service account are the minimum required for cluster operation: 1. Go to IAM by visiting https://console.cloud.google.com/iam-admin/iam 2. From the list of Service accounts, ensure each cluster Service account has only the following roles: • Logs Writer • Monitoring Metric Writer • Monitoring Viewer Using Command line: To check an existing cluster, first define 3 variables for Node Pool, Cluster Name and Zone, and then run the following command: gcloud container node-pools describe $NODE_POOL --cluster $CLUSTER_NAME -- zone $COMPUTE_ZONE --format json | jq '.config.serviceAccount' The output of the above command will return default if default Service account is used or the specific account being used for Project access. To check that the permissions allocated to the service account are the minimum required for cluster operation: gcloud projects get-iam-policy \ --flatten="bindings[].members" \ --format='table(bindings.role)' \ --filter="bindings.members:" Review the output to ensure that the service account only has the roles required to run the cluster: • roles/logging.logWriter • roles/monitoring.metricWriter • roles/monitoring.viewer
input_row.remediation: Using Google Cloud Console: To create a minimally privileged service account: 1. Go to Service Accounts by visiting: https://console.cloud.google.com/iam- admin/serviceaccounts. 2. Click on CREATE SERVICE ACCOUNT. 3. Enter Service Account Details. Internal Only - General 4. Click CREATE AND CONTINUE. 5. Within Service Account permissions add the following roles: o Logs Writer. o Monitoring Metric Writer. o `Monitoring Viewer. 6. Click CONTINUE. 7. Grant users access to this service account and create keys as required. 8. Click DONE. To create a Node pool to use the Service account: 1. Go to Kubernetes Engine by visiting: https://console.cloud.google.com/kubernetes/list. 2. Click on the cluster name within which the Node pool will be launched. 3. Click on ADD NODE POOL. 4. Within the Node Pool details, select the Security subheading, and under `Identity defaults, select the minimally privileged service account from the Service Account drop-down. 5. Click `CREATE to launch the Node pool. Note: The workloads will need to be migrated to the new Node pool, and the old node pools that use the default service account should be deleted to complete the remediation. Using Command Line: To create a minimally privileged service account: gcloud iam service-accounts create --display-name "GKE Node Service Account" export NODE_SA_EMAIL=gcloud iam service-accounts list --format='value(email)' --filter='displayName:GKE Node Service Account' Grant the following roles to the service account: export PROJECT_ID=gcloud config get-value project gcloud projects add-iam-policy-binding --member serviceAccount: --role roles/monitoring.metricWriter gcloud projects add-iam-policy-binding --member serviceAccount: --role roles/monitoring.viewer gcloud projects add-iam-policy-binding --member serviceAccount: --role roles/logging.logWriter To create a new Node pool using the Service account, run the following command: gcloud container node-pools create --service- account=@.iam.gserviceaccount.com-- cluster= --zone Note: The workloads will need to be migrated to the new Node pool, and the old node pools that use the default service account should be deleted to complete the remediation. Internal Only - General
input_row.impact: Instances are automatically granted the https://www.googleapis.com/auth/cloud- platform scope to allow full access to all Google Cloud APIs. This is so that the IAM permissions of the instance are completely determined by the IAM roles of the Service account. Thus if Kubernetes workloads were using cluster access scopes to perform actions using Google APIs, they may no longer be able to, if not permitted by the permissions of the Service account. To remediate, follow recommendation 5.2.2. The Service account roles listed here are the minimum required to run the cluster. Additional roles may be required to pull from a private instance of Google Container Registry (GCR).
input_row.default_value: By default, nodes use the Compute Engine default service account when you create a new cluster.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**PROGRAM NAME:** gcp_gke_cluster_node_service_account_minimally_privileged

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
1. **GCP API Calls / Google Cloud SDK (gcloud) methods:**
   - Use `gcloud container node-pools describe` to retrieve information about the node pools, specifically the service account in use for each pool.
     - Command: `gcloud container node-pools describe $NODE_POOL --cluster $CLUSTER_NAME --zone $COMPUTE_ZONE --format json`
   - Use `gcloud projects get-iam-policy` to fetch the IAM policies related to the service accounts to determine their assigned roles.
     - Command: `gcloud projects get-iam-policy $PROJECT_ID --flatten="bindings[].members" --format='table(bindings.role)' --filter="bindings.members:<service_account_email>"`
  
2. **Resources to enumerate:**
   - Node pools in Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE) clusters and their associated service accounts.
   - Service accounts and their IAM roles.

3. **Exact configuration properties/settings to validate:**
   - Ensure that the service account associated with each GKE node pool is not the default service account and verify its assigned roles.

4. **Pass/Fail criteria:**
   - **Pass:** Each node pool should have a user-defined service account (not the default) with only the following roles: 
     - `roles/logging.logWriter`
     - `roles/monitoring.metricWriter`
     - `roles/monitoring.viewer`
   - **Fail:** If any node pool uses the default service account or has additional or insufficient roles assigned.

5. **Example:**
   - The following command can be used to validate if the service account is default or not:
     ```
     gcloud container node-pools describe $NODE_POOL --cluster $CLUSTER_NAME --zone $COMPUTE_ZONE --format json | jq '.config.serviceAccount'
     ```
   - Confirm the associated roles of the service account:
     ```
     gcloud projects get-iam-policy $PROJECT_ID --flatten="bindings[].members" --format='table(bindings.role)' --filter="bindings.members:<service_account_email>"
     ```
step2_review:
### APPROACH VALIDATION: PARTIALLY_AGREE

- **Manual vs Automated Classification:** The classification as Automated is mostly correct, as the use of `gcloud` commands can automate the retrieval and validation of service accounts and their roles. However, the final assessment of whether the roles are minimally privileged might require some manual interpretation, especially if roles beyond the specified ones are found.
- **GCP APIs/Google Cloud SDK (gcloud)/CLI:** The approach can be checked using GCP APIs and Google Cloud SDK (gcloud) commands, which are well-suited for this task.

### TECHNICAL ACCURACY: 7

- **Automation Details:**
  - The use of `gcloud container node-pools describe` is correct for retrieving node pool configurations, including the service account in use.
  - The `gcloud projects get-iam-policy` command is appropriate for listing IAM roles associated with service accounts, but the filtering logic could be more precise.
- **Program Name:** The program name `gcp_gke_cluster_node_service_account_minimally_privileged` is descriptive but could be more concise. It does follow the convention but could be improved for clarity.
- **Commands Validity:** The commands provided are valid and correctly formatted for their intended purpose.

### IMPROVEMENTS:

- **Filtering Logic:** The filtering in the IAM policy command could be improved by directly checking for the presence of the default service account email pattern, such as `*-compute@developer.gserviceaccount.com`.
- **Role Verification:** Consider automating the role verification by checking if the roles are exactly the ones specified (`roles/logging.logWriter`, `roles/monitoring.metricWriter`, `roles/monitoring.viewer`) and no others.
- **Program Name Suggestion:** Consider renaming to `gcp_gke_node_pool_service_account_check` for brevity and clarity.
- **Additional Considerations:** Include checks for any custom roles that might have equivalent permissions to the specified roles, as these could also be acceptable.

### CONFIDENCE: MEDIUM

- The assessment is generally correct, but the reliance on manual interpretation for role verification and the potential for misconfiguration in the filtering logic slightly reduce confidence.

### FINAL RECOMMENDATION: MODIFY

- **Modify:** Improve the filtering logic in the IAM policy command to specifically check for default service account patterns and ensure the role verification is automated to match only the specified roles.
- **Re-classify:** Consider adding a note that while the initial data retrieval is automated, some manual interpretation may be needed to fully assess compliance, especially in complex IAM configurations.

step3_final_decision.decision_text:
**FINAL APPROACH:** Automated

**JUSTIFICATION:** The task of verifying that GKE clusters are not using the Compute Engine default service account can be effectively automated using the Google Cloud SDK (gcloud) commands. Both AI opinions agree that the necessary information can be retrieved and validated using `gcloud` commands, which can check the service accounts associated with node pools and their IAM roles. The automation can precisely determine compliance by checking if the service account is not the default and if it has the specified roles.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

--- IF AUTOMATED ---

**PROGRAM NAME:** 
gcp_gke_node_pool_service_account_privilege_check

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- GCP Resource/Service: Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE) Node Pools
- API/CLI Method: 
  - `gcloud container node-pools describe` to retrieve node pool configurations.
  - `gcloud projects get-iam-policy` to list IAM roles associated with service accounts.
- Properties to Check: 
  - Service account associated with each node pool.
  - IAM roles assigned to the service account.
- Pass Criteria: 
  - Each node pool uses a user-defined service account (not the default) with roles: `roles/logging.logWriter`, `roles/monitoring.metricWriter`, `roles/monitoring.viewer`.
- Fail Criteria: 
  - Any node pool uses the default service account or has additional/insufficient roles.

**DISAGREEMENTS RESOLVED:** The second opinion suggested a need for manual interpretation if roles beyond the specified ones are found. However, the automation can be designed to flag any deviations from the specified roles, making manual interpretation unnecessary for compliance checks.

**SOURCE:** CIS_Google_Kubernetes_Engine__GKE__Benchmark_v1_8_0_PDF
